abridged.content:
The cardiac cycle - one beat as pressure-driven valve movement

The cardiac cycle is one complete heartbeat: ventricular filling, atrial contribution, ventricular contraction, ventricular ejection, ventricular relaxation, and refilling. It is triggered electrically by the ECG, but the useful bedside physiology is mechanical. Valves open and close because pressure gradients change, not because the ECG directly opens them.

Diastole is the filling phase. It begins after aortic and pulmonary valve closure and includes isovolumetric relaxation, early rapid ventricular filling, diastasis, and atrial systole. Systole is the contraction and ejection phase. It begins after mitral and tricuspid valve closure and includes isovolumetric contraction and ventricular ejection.

During diastole, the mitral and tricuspid valves open once ventricular pressure falls below atrial pressure. During systole, the AV valves close once ventricular pressure exceeds atrial pressure. The aortic and pulmonary valves open once ventricular pressure exceeds aortic or pulmonary artery pressure. They close when ventricular pressure falls below arterial pressure. The dicrotic notch on the arterial waveform represents aortic valve closure.

In CTICU, the cardiac cycle explains why rhythm, rate, preload, afterload, contractility, valve function, pacing, tamponade, ventilation, and IABP timing all matter. An organised ECG rhythm does not guarantee useful mechanical output. The arterial waveform, pulse pressure, CVP, PA pressures, cardiac index, lactate, urine output, echo, and venous saturation tell you whether each beat is producing effective forward flow.

expanded.content:
The cardiac cycle - electrical trigger, mechanical pressure event

The cardiac cycle is best understood as a sequence of pressure gradients. The ECG is important because it initiates atrial and ventricular depolarisation, but the valves respond to pressure, not directly to electricity. This distinction is essential in CTICU because patients can have an apparently organised electrical rhythm while mechanical output is poor. A paced QRS, sinus rhythm, or narrow-complex rhythm does not prove that the ventricle is filling adequately, generating enough pressure, ejecting effectively, or relaxing properly.

The cycle has two broad phases: diastole and systole. Diastole is the period of ventricular relaxation and filling. Systole is the period of ventricular contraction and ejection. The mechanical events are repeated continuously, but the exact timing changes with heart rate, rhythm, ventricular compliance, afterload, contractility, valve disease, ventilation, and pacing mode.

Late diastole and atrial systole

Late diastole occurs when the ventricles are relaxed and filling. The mitral and tricuspid valves are open because atrial pressure exceeds ventricular pressure. The aortic and pulmonary valves are closed because arterial pressure exceeds ventricular pressure. Most ventricular filling is passive. Atrial systole then provides the final active contribution to ventricular filling, commonly called the atrial kick.

Atrial kick is especially important when the ventricle is stiff. In a young, compliant ventricle, passive filling may be enough for acceptable stroke volume. In a hypertrophied LV after aortic stenosis, a post-bypass oedematous ventricle, a diastolic dysfunction patient, or a valve surgery patient, the atrial contribution can be critical. New atrial fibrillation after cardiac surgery removes organised atrial contraction and often shortens filling time because the ventricular rate becomes fast. The result can be a sudden fall in stroke volume, pulse pressure, MAP, urine output, and SvO₂.

This is why new post-operative AF is not just an arrhythmia label. It is a mechanical filling problem. In a patient with stiff ventricles, loss of AV synchrony can convert a borderline circulation into low cardiac output syndrome. The deterioration is not mysterious: the ventricle starts each beat with less useful filling, so ejection falls even when the ECG continues to show ventricular activity.

Isovolumetric contraction

After ventricular depolarisation, seen as the QRS complex, ventricular pressure rises. When LV pressure exceeds LA pressure, the mitral valve closes. When RV pressure exceeds RA pressure, the tricuspid valve closes. This produces the first heart sound, S1. At this point all valves are closed, so ventricular volume does not change even though pressure rises. This is isovolumetric contraction.

The word isovolumetric means same volume. The ventricles are contracting, but no blood is leaving yet. The LV must build enough pressure to exceed aortic diastolic pressure before the aortic valve opens. The RV must build enough pressure to exceed pulmonary artery pressure before the pulmonary valve opens. This phase reflects pressure-generating capacity. Poor contractility, acidosis, hypothermia, hypocalcaemia, myocardial stunning, ischaemia, or excessive afterload can all make pressure generation slower or weaker.

Clinically, poor pressure generation may appear as a weak or slow arterial upstroke once ejection begins, narrow pulse pressure, low cardiac index, cool peripheries, rising lactate, low SvO₂, and increasing vasoactive support. In severe mitral regurgitation, isovolumetric contraction may be functionally altered because blood regurgitates backward into the LA as LV pressure rises.

Ventricular ejection

Ventricular ejection begins when LV pressure exceeds aortic pressure and the aortic valve opens. On the right side, it begins when RV pressure exceeds pulmonary artery pressure and the pulmonary valve opens. The AV valves remain closed. Blood is ejected into the aorta and pulmonary artery. This is the phase that produces the arterial waveform upstroke and stroke volume.

The arterial upstroke is a bedside expression of ventricular ejection. A brisk upstroke suggests effective ejection and/or low impedance. A slow-rising upstroke suggests low LV contractility, severe aortic stenosis physiology, low stroke volume, excessive afterload, or an overdamped arterial line. A narrow pulse pressure can indicate reduced stroke volume from hypovolaemia, tamponade, poor contractility, RV failure with LV underfilling, high afterload, or arterial line damping. A wide pulse pressure may indicate vasoplegia, aortic regurgitation, high stroke volume, or underdamping.

Stroke volume is produced during ejection, but it is determined by what happened before and during ejection: preload, contractility, and afterload. An underfilled ventricle ejects little because it starts with too little volume. A stunned ventricle ejects poorly because it cannot generate enough force. A severely afterloaded ventricle ejects poorly because the resistance is too high. A compressed ventricle in tamponade ejects poorly because filling is mechanically restricted.

Aortic valve closure and isovolumetric relaxation

As ventricular contraction ends, LV pressure falls. When LV pressure falls below aortic pressure, the aortic valve closes. This generates the dicrotic notch on the arterial waveform and contributes to the second heart sound, S2. The pulmonary valve closes similarly on the right side. After semilunar valve closure, all valves are again closed. Ventricular pressure falls without a change in volume. This is isovolumetric relaxation.

Once LV pressure falls below LA pressure, the mitral valve opens and LV filling begins. Once RV pressure falls below RA pressure, the tricuspid valve opens and RV filling begins. This completes the mechanical loop and starts the next diastolic filling phase.

Diastole is clinically active, not passive downtime. Most LV coronary perfusion occurs during diastole because intramyocardial pressure compresses coronary vessels during systole. Tachycardia shortens diastole disproportionately. Therefore, a heart rate of 130-150/min in a fresh CABG, valve, or stiff-ventricle patient can reduce LV filling, reduce coronary perfusion time, increase myocardial oxygen demand, and lower stroke volume.

Valve timing summary

Cycle phaseMitral/tricuspid valvesAortic/pulmonary valvesMain mechanical event
Late diastoleOpenClosedPassive ventricular filling plus atrial systole.
Isovolumetric contractionClosedClosedVentricular pressure rises without ejection.
Ventricular ejectionClosedOpenBlood is ejected into the aorta and pulmonary artery.
Isovolumetric relaxationClosedClosedVentricular pressure falls after semilunar valve closure.
Early fillingOpenClosedVentricles refill once ventricular pressure falls below atrial pressure.

CTICU interpretation of cycle failure

Different clinical problems impair different parts of the cycle. Hypovolaemia, tachycardia, tamponade, high intrathoracic pressure, and RV failure impair filling. Myocardial stunning, ischaemia, hypothermia, acidosis, hypocalcaemia, and excessive afterload impair pressure generation and ejection. Valve obstruction impairs forward ejection. Regurgitant lesions waste stroke volume by allowing backward flow. Loss of atrial kick impairs late filling. Pacing dyssynchrony can impair both filling and ejection.

The monitor rarely labels the failing phase. You infer it from the pattern. Falling MAP with rising CVP and narrowing pulse pressure after cardiac surgery suggests obstructive or RV physiology until proven otherwise. Low MAP with warm peripheries, wide pulse pressure, and high CI suggests vasoplegia. Low CI with high SVR suggests pressure without flow. New rapid AF with falling pulse pressure suggests loss of filling and diastolic time.

The key CTICU skill is to identify which part of the cycle is failing and why. Treating the number without identifying the mechanism can worsen the patient. Fluid helps if the ventricle is preload responsive and tolerant. Inotropy helps if contractility is inadequate. Vasopressor helps if vascular tone is too low. Afterload reduction helps if resistance is excessive. Surgical escalation is required if tamponade, graft failure, valve obstruction, or bleeding is the cause.

For bedside practice, always connect the phase of the cycle to the intervention you are considering. If filling is impaired, the answer may be rhythm optimisation, reduced intrathoracic pressure, relief of tamponade, cautious preload support, or RV rescue. If ejection is impaired, the answer may be inotropy, afterload adjustment, correction of acidosis or electrolytes, investigation for ischaemia, or mechanical support. If relaxation and diastolic time are impaired, the answer may be rate control, analgesia, temperature control, improved synchrony, or reduction of oxygen demand. This phase-based approach prevents reflex treatment and keeps the nurse focused on the mechanism that is actually limiting forward flow.

pearls.content:
Clinical Pearls - The Cardiac Cycle

Pearl 1 - The ECG is not the circulation

An organised ECG rhythm does not prove effective mechanical output. A paced QRS or sinus rhythm can exist with poor filling, poor ejection, tamponade, RV failure, severe afterload, or low stroke volume.

Pearl 2 - Valves are pressure-gradient devices

Mitral and tricuspid valves close when ventricular pressure exceeds atrial pressure. Aortic and pulmonary valves open when ventricular pressure exceeds arterial pressure. This is why preload, afterload, compliance, valve disease, and ventilation change valve timing.

Pearl 3 - The dicrotic notch is aortic valve closure

The dicrotic notch marks aortic valve closure. It is useful for arterial waveform interpretation and IABP timing. If it is absent or distorted, check low flow, damping, low SVR, artefact, and aortic valve physiology.

Pearl 4 - Diastole is where filling and LV coronary perfusion happen

Tachycardia shortens diastole and can reduce ventricular filling and coronary perfusion. This is especially dangerous in CABG, valve, hypertrophied LV, stunned myocardium, and RV failure patients.

Pearl 5 - Atrial kick is not optional in stiff ventricles

New AF after cardiac surgery can sharply reduce stroke volume because atrial systole is lost and diastolic filling time shortens. This is a mechanical problem, not just an electrical rhythm issue.

equipment.content:
Equipment Tie-In - The Cardiac Cycle

Arterial line

The arterial line displays the pressure consequence of ventricular ejection. The upstroke reflects LV ejection. The systolic peak reflects stroke volume, contractility, arterial compliance, and vascular tone. The dicrotic notch reflects aortic valve closure. The downstroke reflects arterial runoff during diastole.

FindingCycle meaningPossible causeImmediate check
Slow upstrokeDelayed or weak ejection.Low contractility, low SV, aortic stenosis, excessive afterload, overdamping.Check pulse, damping, transducer, CO/CI, echo, valve history.
Narrow pulse pressureReduced stroke volume.Hypovolaemia, tamponade, LV failure, RV failure, high afterload, damping.Check drains, CVP trend, lactate, UO, echo, line quality.
Wide pulse pressureHigh SV or low diastolic resistance.Vasoplegia, aortic regurgitation, high output, underdamping.Check diastolic pressure, SVR, warmth, flush test.
Absent notchAortic closure not clearly transmitted.Low flow, damping, low SVR, artefact, valve issue.Check waveform, square wave test, NIBP correlation.

Pacing boxes

Temporary pacing changes the timing of atrial and ventricular activation. AAI preserves atrial timing if AV conduction is intact. VVI maintains ventricular rate but may lose atrial contribution. DDD attempts to coordinate atrial and ventricular timing. The haemodynamic effect depends on rate, AV synchrony, capture, sensing, and ventricular activation pattern.

IABP

The IABP must be timed to the cardiac cycle. Inflation should occur at the dicrotic notch after aortic valve closure to augment diastolic pressure and coronary perfusion. Deflation should occur just before systole to reduce LV afterload. Early or late timing directly interferes with the mechanical cycle.

targets.content:
Targets and Numbers - The Cardiac Cycle

The cardiac cycle is managed by ensuring that each beat has enough filling time, coordinated atrial and ventricular timing, adequate ventricular pressure generation, effective ejection, and enough diastolic time for coronary perfusion. There is no single cardiac-cycle target. The pattern across rate, rhythm, MAP, pulse pressure, CI, filling pressures, lactate, urine output, and venous saturation determines whether the cycle is effective.

In many stable post-cardiac surgery patients, a heart rate around 70-90/min is physiologically useful because it balances cardiac output with diastolic filling and coronary perfusion time. A MAP around 65-85 mmHg is common, but the correct target depends on CABG graft perfusion, valve repair protection, aortic surgery, chronic hypertension, neurological risk, and bleeding risk.

tables.heading: Cardiac cycle bedside parameters
rows: Determines cycle frequency and filling time
Too slow may reduce CO; too fast may reduce filling and coronary perfusion
Sinus rhythm preferred when atrial contribution is important
Coordinates atrial systole before ventricular systole
New AF can remove atrial kick and reduce stroke volume
Pressure generated by flow and vascular tone
Normal MAP does not prove adequate flow
Stroke volume and arterial compliance clue
Narrowing suggests low SV or damping
Interpret with lactate, UO, SvO₂, skin perfusion
Rising CVP with shock suggests RV failure, tamponade, PEEP, tension physiology, or congestion
Global oxygen extraction after the cardiac cycle

quizQuestions.question: What does the dicrotic notch on an arterial waveform represent?
quizQuestions.explanation: The dicrotic notch represents aortic valve closure as LV pressure falls below aortic pressure. It marks the end of ventricular ejection and the start of diastole.

quizQuestions.question: Why can new rapid AF after valve surgery cause hypotension?
options: Loss of atrial kick and shortened diastolic filling time reduce stroke volume
quizQuestions.explanation: Rapid AF removes coordinated atrial systole and shortens diastole. In stiff post-operative ventricles, this reduces filling and stroke volume.

quizQuestions.question: A paced ECG at 80/min is present but pulse pressure is narrow, lactate is rising, and SvO₂ is falling. What is the safest interpretation?
options: Electrical activity is organised but mechanical output may be inadequate
Pacing spikes prove stroke volume is normal
Narrow pulse pressure always means underdamping
quizQuestions.explanation: Electrical rhythm does not guarantee mechanical output. Narrow pulse pressure, rising lactate, and falling SvO₂ suggest inadequate stroke volume or oxygen delivery.
